steps.title: Overview: Settings Vocabulary
steps.description: The architecture tour showed `createLayout` as the handoff point. This tour covers the values that arrive there: raw URL/control settings, normalized purifier settings, print design presets, filter and fan presets, and workbench state.

steps.title: Preview Modes
steps.description: Preview mode is a small union, not a loose string. The same value drives the tab state, URL encoding, and which renderer data gets prepared.

steps.title: Print Design Model
steps.description: The print design union separates generated Nukit geometry, Corsi-Rosenthal geometry, donut HEPA geometry, and curated static references. Later tours follow each branch into its own kit or preview path.

steps.title: Fan Product Presets
steps.description: Fan presets are product-level data: dimensions, count hints, appearance, power notes, and purchase metadata. This lets the UI show useful help without hard-coding fan copy into the component layer.

steps.title: Filter Product Presets
steps.description: Filter presets do the same job for media. The user can choose a known filter, but custom dimensions still flow through the same settings shape.

steps.title: Raw Settings
steps.description: Raw settings are the boundary format. They preserve URL and control values, including values that still need defaults or canonicalization before geometry is calculated.

steps.title: Normalized Settings
steps.description: Normalized settings replace primitive selectors with resolved objects. After this point, downstream logic can use the chosen fan, filter, cutting settings, and print design without re-looking them up.

steps.title: Workbench State
steps.description: Workbench state is separate from purifier settings. It models UI workflow state: preview tab, fabrication method, controls tab, and selected print volume.

steps.title: Design Settings Memory
steps.description: Switching design remembers the old settings per design id. That is why a user can move between Nukit and Corsi modes without losing the dimensions they had already changed.

steps.title: Quick Check
steps.text: Why does the app keep both raw settings and normalized settings?
answers: Raw settings preserve URL/control values, while normalized settings resolve presets and derived objects
Raw settings are for tests only, while normalized settings are for production
Raw settings are mutable, while normalized settings are always stored in localStorage
Raw settings are only used by the Three.js renderer
steps.explanation: `RawPurifierSettings` starts at line 791, while `PurifierSettings` starts at line 834 and includes resolved objects such as `filter`, `fan`, and `printDesign`.

steps.title: Summary
steps.description: The app separates product vocabulary, raw boundary settings, normalized domain settings, and workflow state. The next tour shows the typed result shapes produced after those settings pass through the domain pipeline.
